segments.title: Fast-firing guns against torpedo boats
segments.summary: The Royal Navy treated E-boat defence as the familiar torpedo-boat problem and answered it with fast-firing guns.
segments.body: Clarke reduces the Royal Navy's response to E-boats to a basic tactical problem: how to stop torpedo boats before they can complete an attack. The compact answer is fast-firing guns. He presents the same principle as relevant to attacks by small boats more generally, including modern examples. The defender needs weapons that can put effective fire onto a small, rapidly moving threat during a short engagement opportunity. This opening establishes gunfire, rather than a matching torpedo attack, as the foundation for the countermeasures discussed in the rest of the video.

evidence.note: The summary identifies fast-firing guns as the answer to torpedo boats and small-boat attacks.

segments.title: Reaction speed against E-boat ambushes
segments.summary: Crowded waters, darkness, small targets, and deception against early radar made reaction speed central to E-boat defence.
segments.body: Clarke notes that engaging a threat at long range is preferable whenever detection permits it. E-boats, however, were intended to behave as ambush predators and deny the defender that comfortable warning distance. Their small size, use of crowded shipping lanes, and night operations could conceal their approach. Even when radar was available, an attacker might try to spoof early sets and preserve surprise. The practical requirement was therefore a very fast defensive reaction, with enough immediate firepower either to drive the boat away or destroy it before its attack developed.

evidence.note: The transcript explains how limited visibility, crowded lanes, and early-radar spoofing demand rapid defensive reaction.

segments.title: Coastal gunboats built to stop E-boats
segments.summary: Steam Gun Boats, Fairmile Ds, and heavily armed small escorts supplied the speed and rapid fire needed for close E-boat engagements.
segments.body: The Royal Navy answered the E-boat threat with numerous small gun-armed craft that could react quickly and bring substantial firepower to the encounter. Clarke distinguishes these from gunboats in the older Insect-class sense, while acknowledging that Insect-class vessels achieved some success against E-boats in a small number of encounters. His main examples are Steam Gun Boats and Fairmile D craft. The essential qualities were rapid deployment and enough gunfire to drive onto an attacker rather than merely endure its approach. He also explains why small escorts could carry more guns than an observer might expect from their apparent role. Some weapons described as anti-aircraft guns were valuable because their rapid fire could also engage an E-boat once it came within range.

evidence.note: The transcript names Steam Gun Boats and Fairmile Ds, and explains that rapid-firing weapons on small escorts could be intended for E-boat defence.
